title: The hidden climate hotspot
subtitle: Small areas, disproportionate emissions
story_text: Drained agricultural peatlands are not only ecological remnants. Where organic soils, drainage and farming overlap, they become climate-relevant production landscapes. The Atlas starts by locating this overlap before asking which transitions are possible.
main_message: Drained agricultural peatlands are climate hotspots because peat, drainage and production overlap.
interaction_note: Start with a global map, then zoom to Europe and South Germany. Toggle peatland extent, drained organic soils, agricultural land cover and emissions if public layers are available.
callout_text: The hotspot is hydrological, agricultural and economic — not only ecological.
portfolio_angle: Creates an accessible opening frame and prepares the shift from hotspot mapping to transition design.
notes: For MVP, use country-level FAOSTAT/FAO data before attempting large raster workflows.

title: The water table is the lever
subtitle: Area alone does not determine mitigation
story_text: Peatland mitigation depends on the water table that can actually be achieved. The same mapped peatland area can lead to different climate, management and production outcomes depending on groundwater level, soil conditions and land-use intensity.
main_message: The decisive variable is not peatland area alone, but achievable water-table depth.
primary_view: Water-table gradient with linked outcome cards
interaction_note: User drags a water-table slider from deeply drained to near-surface conditions. Cards update for CO2, CH4 risk, trafficability, production continuity and feasible land-use pathways.
callout_text: A hectare of peatland is not a uniform mitigation unit.
portfolio_angle: Turns a technical hydrological mechanism into a simple interactive explanation.
notes: Can be implemented with qualitative rules first; quantitative functions can be added later.

title: Evidence from elsewhere
subtitle: International studies as a structured evidence layer
story_text: Different countries contribute different pieces of the transition puzzle: Dutch studies highlight raised groundwater, peat pasture and trafficability; Finnish studies model incentives and compensation; Danish studies test biomass pathways; German and UK studies add adoption, governance and productive-peatland perspectives.
main_message: International evidence does not provide one solution; it provides transferable lessons.
primary_view: Map with evidence-region nodes and paper cards
interaction_note: Click a region node to open a card with supporting papers, key message, pathway relevance, South Germany transfer note and caveat. Filter by hydrology, economics, biomass, grazing, adoption and governance.
callout_text: The Atlas treats papers as evidence nodes, not as isolated references.
portfolio_angle: Converts a literature review into a navigable evidence map.
notes: This is the best first interactive prototype because all required datasets are already available.

title: The transition space
subtitle: Between drained farming and full restoration
story_text: Rewetting should not be visualised as a binary choice between current farming and complete restoration. The literature describes a transition space: partial water management, raised-water-table pasture, wet mowing, wet biomass, reed value chains, robust grazing and restoration.
main_message: The design space is a spectrum of wetter land-use pathways, each with different trade-offs.
primary_view: Horizontal wetness-gradient diagram plus comparison matrix
interaction_note: User selects a pathway. The matrix highlights GHG potential, water-table requirement, trafficability constraint, farm compatibility, market maturity, adoption barrier and South Germany fit.
callout_text: The key question is what kind of agriculture remains possible under wetter conditions.
portfolio_angle: Provides the central conceptual graphic of the portfolio piece.
notes: Scores are qualitative and must be clearly labelled as literature-informed expert coding.

title: What fits South Germany?
subtitle: From evidence to transfer hypotheses
story_text: Not every wet land-use pathway is equally plausible in South German dairy, forage and biogas regions. The Atlas translates international evidence into transfer hypotheses, asking which pathways might align with hydrology, machinery access, farm structure, markets and adoption conditions.
main_message: South Germany needs transition pathways that connect mitigation with recognisable farm systems.
primary_view: Bubble chart: hydrological requirement × farm compatibility; size = GHG potential; colour = adoption/market risk
interaction_note: Hover on each pathway to show supporting papers and caveats. Toggle criteria: farm compatibility, market maturity, trafficability, adoption barrier.
callout_text: A pathway can be scientifically plausible and still fail without farm compatibility, machinery access or markets.
portfolio_angle: Turns the Atlas into a decision-support style view for regional transition discussion.
notes: A first version can be literature-only; later add Thünen/LGRB/Copernicus regional layers.

title: The modelling frontier
subtitle: Where the atlas meets research infrastructure
story_text: The literature provides building blocks, but local decisions require integrated modelling. Hydrology determines achievable water tables; water tables shape emissions; farm structure shapes economic exposure; and viable pathways determine whether rewetting can become implementable.
main_message: The next decision tools must connect hydrology, GHG mitigation, farm economics and viable land use.
primary_view: Hydrology → water table → GHG mitigation → farm exposure → transition pathway diagram
interaction_note: Click each node to reveal data requirements, current evidence and remaining gaps. Optional: link to PALUD/RoGeR research context without exposing sensitive data.
callout_text: A peatland map is not enough; the decision unit is the interaction of water, emissions, farms and transition options.
portfolio_angle: Connects the public-facing Atlas to future hydrological-economic modelling.
notes: This section can be mostly explanatory in the MVP.
